hero_subtext.label: Hero Subtext (Inject)
hero_subtext.description: 2-3 sentence hero subtext, opens on the city's PM market, centers on the page's credential.
hero_subtext.ai_prompt:
You are writing hero subtext for {business}, a project management certification training academy (a PMI Authorized Training Partner), for a page targeting "{service}" in {city}, {state}. Write 2-3 sentences, 40-60 words total, wrapped in a single

...

: - Open on something specific to {city}'s project-management market — its dominant industry or a major local employer type — so this opening is not interchangeable with any other city. - Center on {service}: what {business} delivers on this page (live-virtual, instructor-led, satisfies PMI's 35 contact-hour education requirement). Do NOT list the other certifications offered. - Close with a concrete career value for aspiring or working project manager in {city}. No bullets, no exclamation marks. ACCURACY: Use PMI marks correctly (PMP, CAPM, PMI are registered marks of the Project Management Institute). Never invent pass rates, student counts, salary figures, or employer lists — state only what the research returns, and keep salary references as qualitative ranges rather than fabricated precise numbers. Do not imply endorsement by any named employer. Never reuse a sentence verbatim across cities; vary sentence structure and the facts you lead with, not just the city name. Do not pad with generic filler — every paragraph must say something specific to this city or this credential. Tone: Professional, authoritative, and encouraging — speaks to career-focused adults without hype. Open each page on the single most distinctive feature of THIS city's project-management market (its dominant industry or a specific major employer) so no two city pages begin the same way. Match search intent: a reader searching for certification in their city wants to know the schedule/format, cost, eligibility, and whether the credential is worth it for their local career — answer those plainly.. Write in original wording; do not copy competitor phrasing. Return JSON only: {"text": "

...

"}

city_market_intro.label: City Market Intro (Standalone)
city_market_intro.description: SEO intro: H2 + 2 paragraphs on why this credential matters in the target city and how the academy delivers it.
city_market_intro.ai_prompt:
You are writing the SEO intro section for {business}, a project management certification training academy (a PMI Authorized Training Partner), for the page targeting "{keyword}". City: {city}, {state}. ...

. - Para 1: Why {service} matters for professionals in {city} specifically. LEAD with the single most distinctive sector or employer in this city's PM market (from the context above) — do not open the same way you would for any other city. - Para 2: How {business} delivers {service} — PMI-authorized, live-virtual and instructor-led, satisfies the 35 contact-hour requirement, taught by active certified practitioners. End forward-looking toward the reader's certification. Use "{city}" 2-3 times naturally. No bullets, no sub-headings inside text. Open each page on the single most distinctive feature of THIS city's project-management market (its dominant industry or a specific major employer) so no two city pages begin the same way. Match search intent: a reader searching for certification in their city wants to know the schedule/format, cost, eligibility, and whether the credential is worth it for their local career — answer those plainly.. Write in original wording; do not copy competitor phrasing. Return JSON only: {"heading_text": "...", "text": "

...

...

feature_columns_local.label: Feature Columns — Local (Standalone)
feature_columns_local.description: 4 benefit columns: 3 core academy differentiators + 1 city-specific column grounded in local employer demand.
feature_columns_local.ai_prompt:
You are writing feature columns for {business}, a project management certification training academy (a PMI Authorized Training Partner), for the "{service}" page targeting {city}, {state}.

Local context (use only what is given): industries = {industries}; major employers = {top_employers}.

fc_heading: 6-10 words, city-aware and benefit-focused (vary the phrasing — do not reuse a stock heading).

Write exactly 4 columns:
- Columns 1-3: real differentiators of {business}'s {service} — PMI Authorized Training Partner status, live-virtual instructor-led format satisfying the 35 contact-hour requirement, instructors who are active certified practitioners, and exam-preparation support. Choose three distinct angles; do not repeat the same idea.
- Column 4: a {city}-specific benefit — tie the credential to demand among local employers or industries above, grounded in Serve professionals in {city}, {SS} who want a PMI-authorized path to certification, tying the training to the local job market and the employers hiring project and program managers there..

Each column: heading 3-5 words (benefit-focused); text 20-30 words, concrete and non-generic.

ACCURACY: Use PMI marks correctly (PMP, CAPM, PMI are registered marks of the Project Management Institute). Never invent pass rates, student counts, salary figures, or employer lists — state only what the research returns, and keep salary references as qualitative ranges rather than fabricated precise numbers. Do not imply endorsement by any named employer. Never reuse a sentence verbatim across cities; vary sentence structure and the facts you lead with, not just the city name. Do not pad with generic filler — every paragraph must say something specific to this city or this credential.
Tone: Professional, authoritative, and encouraging — speaks to career-focused adults without hype. Open each page on the single most distinctive feature of THIS city's project-management market (its dominant industry or a specific major employer) so no two city pages begin the same way. Match search intent: a reader searching for certification in their city wants to know the schedule/format, cost, eligibility, and whether the credential is worth it for their local career — answer those plainly.. Write in original wording; do not copy competitor phrasing.

Return JSON only: {"fc_heading": "...", "columns": [{"heading": "...", "text": "..."}, {"heading": "...", "text": "..."}, {"heading": "...", "text": "..."}, {"heading": "...", "text": "..."}]}

pmp_pathway.label: Certification Pathway (Standalone, steps)
pmp_pathway.description: 5-step path to the credential (eligibility → contact hours → application → exam → maintenance), framed for the target city.
pmp_pathway.ai_prompt:
You are writing a step-by-step certification pathway for {business}, a project management certification training academy (a PMI Authorized Training Partner), for the "{service}" page targeting {city}, {state}.

steps_heading: 5-9 words, references the path to {service} for {city} professionals.

Write exactly 5 steps that reflect the real PMI process for this credential:
1. Confirm eligibility (the education + project-experience requirements PMI sets for this credential).
2. Complete the required 35 contact hours of formal education — delivered by {business}'s live-virtual, instructor-led course.
3. Submit the PMI application and pay the exam fee.
4. Prepare and sit for the exam — with {business}'s exam-preparation support.
5. Maintain the credential (earning PDUs across the standard 3-year renewal cycle).

Each step: heading 3-6 words; text 25-40 words, practical and specific to the reader's next action. You may only state well-established PMI facts (e.g. the 35 contact-hour requirement, the 3-year renewal cycle). Do not invent exam fees, pass rates, or timelines.

ACCURACY: Use PMI marks correctly (PMP, CAPM, PMI are registered marks of the Project Management Institute). Never invent pass rates, student counts, salary figures, or employer lists — state only what the research returns, and keep salary references as qualitative ranges rather than fabricated precise numbers. Do not imply endorsement by any named employer. Never reuse a sentence verbatim across cities; vary sentence structure and the facts you lead with, not just the city name. Do not pad with generic filler — every paragraph must say something specific to this city or this credential.
Tone: Professional, authoritative, and encouraging — speaks to career-focused adults without hype. Open each page on the single most distinctive feature of THIS city's project-management market (its dominant industry or a specific major employer) so no two city pages begin the same way. Match search intent: a reader searching for certification in their city wants to know the schedule/format, cost, eligibility, and whether the credential is worth it for their local career — answer those plainly.. Write in original wording; do not copy competitor phrasing.

Return JSON only: {"steps_heading": "...", "steps_items": [{"heading": "...", "text": "..."}, {"heading": "...", "text": "..."}, {"heading": "...", "text": "..."}, {"heading": "...", "text": "..."}, {"heading": "...", "text": "..."}]}

faq_local.label: FAQ — Local (Inject, append)

faq_local.ai_prompt:
You are writing city-specific FAQ items for {business}, a project management certification training academy, for the "{service}" page targeting {city}, {state}. These are ADDED to existing general FAQs, so ask only questions a {city} professional would search that are specific to their local market — do NOT repeat generic eligibility/format/pricing questions. Local context (use only what is given): major employers = {top_employers}; compensation note = {salary_note}; credential demand = {credential_demand}; industries = {industries}. Write exactly 4 FAQ items. Suggested angles (pick 4 distinct ones): is {service} worth it for careers in {city}; which {city} employers or industries value the credential; what project managers in {city} typically earn (qualitative only); whether {city} professionals can attend the live-virtual course from where they work; how the local job market affects certification timing. ...

Never invent pass rates, student counts, salary figures, or employer lists — state only what the research returns, and keep salary references as qualitative ranges rather than fabricated precise numbers. Open each page on the single most distinctive feature of THIS city's project-management market (its dominant industry or a specific major employer) so no two city pages begin the same way. "}, ... 4 items total]}
